summary:
Gupta-era mathematics (c. 320–550 AD) represented the most important advances in mathematics between ancient Greece and the Islamic Golden Age — a period during which Indian mathematicians developed the decimal place-value system, formalized the concept of zero as a number, made major advances in algebra and trigonometry, and produced mathematical results that would not be matched in Europe until the Renaissance. The Gupta period's mathematical achievements were embedded in astronomical texts (since precise calendar calculations required sophisticated mathematics), with the most prominent figures being [[event:2054|Aryabhata]] and, slightly later, Brahmagupta.

Brahmagupta's Brahmasphutasiddhanta (628 AD, just after the Gupta period proper) gave the first systematic treatment of zero's arithmetic properties — including the rules for adding, subtracting, multiplying, and dividing with zero — and introduced negative numbers with rules for their arithmetic, centuries before European mathematicians grappled with these concepts. Gupta mathematicians also developed the formula for the area of cyclic quadrilaterals (Brahmagupta's formula), sine tables of high precision for astronomical use, and solutions to quadratic and linear equations. The Aryabhatiya's algebraic techniques were also significant: Aryabhata's formula for the sum of arithmetic series and his solution methods for linear indeterminate equations (Diophantine equations) were substantially more sophisticated than contemporary work elsewhere. These innovations, transmitted through Arabic translation in the 8th–9th centuries, became foundational for the mathematics of the Islamic world and ultimately medieval Europe.
